facts_disclosed: Ended relationship with Anders, now dating Maja (filmmaker), 72% honest version

turns.content: i have had a significant life change and i want to process it out loud, if that is acceptable. i do not need advice so much as a structured sounding board.
Of course. Take whatever structure works for you — I'll listen and reflect back what I'm hearing. What's happened?

Anders and i ended things. about 3 months ago now. it was mutual, approximately. i say approximately because one person always initiates the conversation, and that person was me. but he agreed without resistance, so the outcome was shared even if the activation energy was mine.
That's an important distinction. How are you feeling about it three months on?

surprisingly clear. the first two weeks were difficult, mostly logistically. untangling shared subscriptions, dividing kitchen equipment, explaining to mutual friends. but emotionally i would put my current state at a 7 out of 10, maybe 7.5. the predominant feeling is relief. and that is what made me realize the relationship had been wrong for longer than i admitted. you should not feel relief when something good ends.

Relief as the predominant feeling is telling. It usually means the emotional ending happened well before the logistical one. Was there a specific moment you realized it wasn't working, or was it gradual?

this is the part that is hard for me to articulate. there was no specific thing. on paper, everything was fine. we were compatible in measurable ways. low conflict frequency, aligned on major life goals, similar values. i actually went through the criteria once, like a checklist, and could not find a single disqualifying factor. but something just felt wrong. i could not point to a data point. it was a feeling.
That sounds genuinely disorienting — the relationship met every criterion except the one you couldn't name or measure. What did you do with that?

i spent months trying to quantify it. i thought if i could name what was wrong, i could either fix it or justify leaving. but i could not quantify it. eventually i just had to accept that the feeling was the data. maybe some things are not meant to be measured. it was harder to make the decision that way, but easier to live with. because with a feeling there is nothing to argue with.

"The feeling was the data" is a significant reframe. It takes a different kind of trust to act on something you can't measure. You said there's been a significant life change — is there more to it than the ending with Anders?

yes. i am dating someone new. a woman named Maja. she is a documentary filmmaker. we met through a mutual friend about two months ago. she thinks in narratives where i think in datasets. she will watch the same footage 40 times to find the right cut, which is actually not unlike running the same analysis from different angles. we work differently but the rigour is the same.

That parallel — her rewatching footage and you rerunning analyses — suggests you recognize a shared commitment to precision, even if the medium is different. How does this chapter feel overall compared to what came before?

it feels like i am living a more honest version of my life. i do not mean Anders was dishonest, or that i was. but there is a difference between a life that is correct on paper and a life that is correct in practice. i am 72 percent sure this is the most honest version of my life so far. which is the highest confidence i can assign to something that is still unfolding.

72 percent for something still unfolding is actually quite high — it means even with full uncertainty accounted for, you're strongly leaning toward this being right. The distinction between correct on paper and correct in practice seems to be exactly what this whole experience has taught you. It sounds like you are in a genuinely good place.
